ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oolong tea beverage class (FOODON_03301103); no specific entry for Dong Ding variety or honey-sweetened oolong. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers oolong tea as a category; no specific Dong Ding or honey-sweetened variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227313005 covers oolong tea as a substance; honey addition and Dong Ding variety are not separately coded. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard takeaway cup of Dong Ding Oolong Tea with Honey, approximately 350 ml, as served at a Taiwanese tea specialty restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, tea, oolong, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA SR Legacy 14366) combined with honey nutrient data (USDA SR Legacy 19296)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed oolong tea (USDA FDC #14366) provides the tea base nutrients. Honey (USDA FDC #19296) is added at an estimated 15 g per 350 ml serving (approximately 4.3 g per 100 ml of beverage), yielding a blended per-100g profile. The final per-100g values represent the ready-to-drink beverage as served. Energy, carbohydrate, and sugar are primarily contributed by honey; protein, fat, and micronutrients are negligible from the tea base.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; honey GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed oolong tea contains no glycemic carbohydrates and would have a GI of effectively zero. The addition of honey (GI approximately 55–58 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) drives the glycemic response of the beverage. The blended GI is estimated at 55 (low), reflecting honey as the sole glycemic contributor at approximately 15 g per 350 ml serving. Glycemic load per serving = (55 × 17.15 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 9.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~7 mg per 350 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Dong Ding Oolong Tea with Honey is very low in sodium (approximately 7 mg per serving) and poses minimal concern for hypertension; however, individuals should be mindful of total added-sugar intake from the honey component, as excess sugar intake can indirectly contribute to elevated blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Honey-derived sugars contribute approximately 17 g of simple sugars per 350 ml serving with a low glycemic index of ~55
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this beverage with caution; the honey addition contributes approximately 17 g of simple sugars per standard serving (GL ~9.4), which can raise postprandial blood glucose, and portion control or substitution with an unsweetened oolong tea is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and sodium; oolong tea polyphenols may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dong Ding Oolong Tea with Honey contains negligible saturated fat and sodium, and the oolong tea polyphenols (catechins and theaflavins) have been associated with modest improvements in LDL cholesterol and endothelial function in observational studies; however, the honey-derived sugar content should be moderated in individuals managing cardiovascular risk factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dong Ding Oolong Tea with Honey is a Taiwanese-origin brewed tea beverage served as a specialty drink at Taiwanese tea houses and bubble tea establishments in Singapore, including CHICHA San Chen. 'Dong Ding' (凍頂, literally 'Frozen Summit') refers to a high-altitude tea-growing region in Nantou County, Taiwan, where the tea leaves are harvested and subjected to a distinctive charcoal-roasting process that imparts a strong, smoky-sweet aroma with caramel undertones. The tea is semi-oxidised (approximately 15–40% oxidation), placing it between green and black tea on the oxidation spectrum. When brewed, it yields a golden-amber liquor with a smooth, full-bodied mouthfeel, a lingering roasted finish, and natural floral-fruity notes. The addition of honey — typically a polyfloral or acacia honey — adds sweetness and rounds out the roasted bitterness, contributing approximately 15 g of honey per 350 ml serving. Nutritionally, the beverage is low in energy (~65 kcal per 350 ml serving), contains negligible protein and fat, and derives its carbohydrate content (~17 g per serving) almost entirely from honey-derived fructose and glucose. The tea base provides trace amounts of potassium, fluoride, and polyphenolic antioxidants including EGCG and theaflavins. Clinically, the beverage is low in sodium and saturated fat, making it broadly suitable for most dietary patterns, but the honey-derived simple sugars warrant moderation for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing glycaemic load. Oolong tea polyphenols have been studied for potential benefits in lipid metabolism and blood glucose regulation, though the honey addition partially offsets these benefits in a sweetened preparation.

confidence_reason: No direct nutrient entry for Dong Ding Oolong Tea with Honey exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for plain brewed oolong tea (FDC #14366) and honey (FDC #19296) at a representative serving ratio. GI is estimated from honey GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data (plain brewed oolong tea + honey at ~15 g per 350 ml serving). No exact match exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or other primary regional sources. 2. protein_g is naturally zero as brewed oolong tea contains no protein and honey contributes negligible protein below detection threshold in a diluted beverage. 3. fat_g is naturally zero as both brewed oolong tea and diluted honey solution contain no fat. 4. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are naturally zero for the same reason. 5. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as brewed tea is a filtered liquid with no fibre. 6. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as this is a plant-derived beverage. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived beverage with no animal-derived components. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero as neither tea nor honey contains vitamin D. 9. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy for brewed oolong tea or honey at this dilution level. 10. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are -1 as values are below reliable reporting thresholds or not reported in the source database for this diluted beverage. 11. glycemic_index set to 55 (low) based on honey GI from University of Sydney GI Database; plain oolong tea base contributes no glycemic carbohydrates. 12. Serving size of 350 g used as representative of a standard takeaway cup in Singapore. 13. The food is not vegan as honey is an animal-derived product. 14. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301103 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for Dong Ding honey-sweetened oolong. 15. SNOMED CT 227313005 is a closest-parent match for oolong tea substance.
